content:
{
  "title": "Program Sketch Prompting: Structuring Prompts with High-Level Code Skeletons for Improved LLM Code Generation",
  "problem": "Current prompting techniques for LLMs often fail to guide models toward desired code structure, resulting in syntactically valid but semantically incomplete or incorrect code. Improving prompt structure to better control high-level code organization is a key challenge.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs become increasingly used for code generation in practical scenarios, users demand not just correctness but code that adheres to intended logical structure and idioms. Existing methods such as few-shot or instruction prompting offer limited control over structure, and improved prompting can unlock higher reliability in real-world software engineering tasks.",
  "method": "We propose 'Program Sketch Prompting' (PSP), where the prompt includes a high-level code skeleton—an outline with placeholders for key functions, classes, or logic blocks—alongside the natural-language specification. The LLM is instructed to complete the code by filling in the placeholders, thus constraining generation toward a user-defined architecture. We introduce an automated tool to generate program sketches (via static analysis or template mining) and test both manual and tool-generated sketches as part of the prompt. The key novelty is conditioning LLM outputs on explicit, partial structure rather than solely on example completions or language instructions.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate PSP on HumanEval and MBPP datasets, comparing against vanilla instruction prompting and advanced chain-of-thought prompting as baselines. Metrics include functional correctness (pass@k), code similarity to reference, and structural fidelity to the provided sketch. We will conduct ablations to test manual versus automated sketch quality, and measure improvements in correctness and structural adherence."
}
